transcript.clean_text: Stage three of the Tour de Romandie, 165.1 rolling kilometers from Valbroye to Valbroye. There we go, the route for today containing six categorized climbs. Just south of this beautiful lake, multiple changes of direction. One passage through the finish and another loop around. That is the challenge facing the riders on this particular stage. Two third category climbs within the last 20 km just to spice things up a little bit. The man in the race lead, in that green jersey, Rohan Dennis of Jumbo-Visma, looking relaxed and relatively tranquil at the start. A breakaway soon went clear, Rémi Cavagna, Nans Peters and Chris Hamiltons. Quick-Step Alpha Vinyl, Israel Premier Tech and AG2R all represented in the front group. And meanwhile, the pace setting, unsurprisingly, been set by Jumbo-Visma behind. Chris Hamiltons was having a very, very good day for Israel Premier Tech. Took every single one of the climbs while the breakaway were clear and moved himself into a comfortable lead in the King of the Mountains classification. And meanwhile, there were some extra troops on the front of the bunch chasing hard. Mathias Flückiger unfortunately came to grief, touched wheels towards the back of the bunch, nearly took out a rider from Groupama-FDJ, but he was up and safely back in the bunch once more. The chase behind though was increasing. The pace ratcheting up and eventually with just under 20 km to go, the break was brought back to heel. Nans Peters with a final roll of the dice perhaps to fight for the combativity award. And onto the final climb of the day, of the climb of Sidera, there was an attack from Carlos Verona. He surged clear on one of the flatter sections and opened up a lead of around 9 seconds with 2 km from the top of the climb still to go. This sparked off several counterattacks. The first of which was from Gino Mäder of Bahrain Victorious. The Swiss rider opening up a little bit of a gap and taking with him Alexandr Vlasov of Bora-Hansgrohe. Then on the descent of that climb, it was all back together. Around 40 riders in the lead group including the race leader Rohan Dennis. Then this man surged clear, Rein Taaramäe of Intermarché-Wanty-Gobert opened up a little gap on the run into the line, but with 500 m to go, he was caught by a surging Mauro Schmid of Quick-Step Alpha Vinyl into the final 400 m and the man that stepped on the gas first and with most effectiveness was this man here, Paddy Bevin of Israel Premier Tech took the stage by a couple of lengths in the end. His first World Tour win of the year, win number three for him, but importantly, the first World Tour win for Israel Premier Tech and they looked absolutely delighted. These are the final moments again coming into that right-hander with 400 m to go. Ethan Hayter in second position in orange, but look at the crisp, clean, brutally effective acceleration there by the man from New Zealand. Nothing anybody could do about it. Quick launch round, victory was his. Hayter in second place and a fine ride too by the man in the green jersey, Rohan Dennis for third. He picks up a 4-second time bonus. So, the stage win to Paddy Bevin, Ethan Hayter in second, Rohan Dennis in third, Dion Smith in fourth, Quentin Hermans fifth, then Caruso, Fisher-Black, Grossschartner, Honoré, Michael Honoré in 10th place. Surprises galore from the man from New Zealand. He looked absolutely delighted. A big, big win to add to his form this year. So, the GC heading into the queen stage, Rohan Dennis still leads with a 14-second advantage over Paddy Bevin. Then we have Felix Grossschartner in third, Schmid, Turns, Ayuso, Honoré, McNulty, Vlasov and Mark Hirschy rounding out the top 10. Make sure you stick with us for stage four, the queen stage of this race. We start in Aigle and we have a mountain top finish in Zeinal. 180 km, six categorized climbs and 4,000 m of elevation.
